idioms.idiom: a leopard can't change its spots
idioms.definition: A person cannot change their fundamental character, nature, or habits, no matter how hard they try
examples: He promised to be more punctual, but a leopard can't change its spots.
Don't expect him to suddenly become generous – a leopard can't change its spots.
She hoped therapy would transform him, but as they say, a leopard can't change its spots.
idioms.usageTip: Often used sceptically or pessimistically about someone who has repeatedly failed to change. It implies the person's behaviour is deeply ingrained.

idioms.definition: To maintain your opinion or position despite pressure or opposition; to refuse to give in
examples: Despite the criticism, she stuck to her guns and published the report.
He stuck to his guns even when the whole board disagreed with him.
If you believe you're right, stick to your guns.
idioms.usageTip: Can be admirable (showing conviction) or stubborn (refusing to listen), depending on context. Pay attention to tone.

idioms.definition: Completely controlled or dominated by someone else; having no independence of action or thought
examples: He was completely under his manager's thumb and never questioned any decision.
She finally left the relationship after realising she had been under his thumb for years.
The department is under the director's thumb – no one dares to disagree.
idioms.usageTip: Usually has a negative connotation – the person under the thumb lacks autonomy. Often used in relationship or workplace contexts.

idioms.definition: To do something in a completely new and different way; to be original or different from what is usual or expected
examples: With her unconventional designs, she really broke the mould in the fashion industry.
The new CEO broke the mould by making the board meetings completely transparent.
This film really breaks the mould of the typical superhero genre.
idioms.usageTip: Used to praise originality or innovation. Suggests that the previous 'mould' (template) no longer applies. American spelling: 'break the mold'.

idioms.idiom: give sb a taste of their own medicine
idioms.definition: To treat someone in the same unpleasant way that they have been treating others
examples: He finally got a taste of his own medicine when the new manager treated him the same way he had treated others.
She decided to give him a taste of his own medicine and ignored his calls.
Sometimes people need a taste of their own medicine to understand how their behaviour affects others.
idioms.usageTip: Implies a sense of justice or revenge. The 'medicine' is unpleasant – it's the same bad treatment being returned.

idioms.definition: To make someone feel too proud or arrogant as a result of success or praise; to lose humility
examples: The sudden fame really went to his head.
She's been promoted twice and it seems to have gone to her head.
Don't let the applause go to your head – there's still a lot of work to do.
idioms.usageTip: Also used for alcohol: 'That wine is going to my head'. In the context of success or praise, it always implies the person has become arrogant or overly confident.

fillInExercises.sentence: He's been rude to the team for years, and now the new system limits his authority. Some say he's finally getting ___.
options: a taste of his own medicine
fillInExercises.correct: a taste of his own medicine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Give sb a taste of their own medicine' means to treat someone the same way they have been treating others.

fillInExercises.sentence: Despite considerable pressure to revise her findings, the scientist ___.
options: gave them a taste of their own medicine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Stick to your guns' means to maintain your position despite criticism or opposition.

fillInExercises.sentence: His first novel was a bestseller, and unfortunately the success seemed to ___.
options: give him a taste of his own medicine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Go to your head' means that success makes someone arrogant or overly proud.

fillInExercises.sentence: He'll never stop gambling – ___. He's been doing it his whole life.
options: a leopard can't change its spots
fillInExercises.correct: a leopard can't change its spots
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A leopard can't change its spots' means a person cannot fundamentally change their nature.

fillInExercises.sentence: Her design approach ___ in the architecture world, challenging every conventional principle.
options: gave her a taste of her own medicine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Break the mould' means to do something in a completely original or unprecedented way.

fillInExercises.sentence: Everyone in the department knows he's completely ___ – he won't make a single decision without her approval.
options: giving her a taste of her own medicine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Under sb's thumb' means completely controlled or dominated by someone else.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Did you hear? Marcus has apparently completely changed since he moved to the countryside.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: Hmm. I'm not sure I believe it. You know what they say – [a leopard can't change its spots|a leopard can't change its spots].

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: That's a bit cynical. People can grow.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: True. And to be fair, he really [broke the mould|break the mould] when he left the corporate world. Nobody expected that.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Exactly. He used to be [under the company's thumb|under sb's thumb] completely – never challenged anything.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: And now he's writing articles criticising the industry and really [sticking to his guns|stick to your guns] despite the backlash.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Let's hope the attention doesn't [go to his head|go to your head]. That was always his weakness before.

lines.speaker: Leon
lines.text: Agreed. Staying grounded will be his biggest challenge.

lines.speaker: Yuki
lines.text: I can't believe the director dismissed your proposal without even reading it.

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: It's infuriating. But I intend to [stick to my guns|stick to your guns] and present it again next quarter.

lines.speaker: Yuki
lines.text: Good for you. Don't let them intimidate you. You know they've been treating junior staff terribly.

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: Sometimes I think they need [a taste of their own medicine|give sb a taste of their own medicine]. Maybe then they'd understand the impact.

lines.speaker: Yuki
lines.text: Half the team is completely [under his thumb|under sb's thumb]. It's not a healthy dynamic.

lines.speaker: Finn
lines.text: I know. But I won't be. The promotion last year seems to have [gone to his head|go to your head].

lines.speaker: Yuki
lines.text: He used to be great – but [a leopard can't change its spots|a leopard can't change its spots], I suppose, once power is involved.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.idiom: a leopard can't change its spots
pairs.meaning: people cannot change their fundamental character
do something original, departing from convention

pairs.idiom: give sb a taste of their own medicine
pairs.meaning: treat someone the same unpleasant way they treat others
make someone arrogant as a result of success

quizExercises.sentence: The young architect's work completely ___ – nothing like it had been seen before.
options: gave them a taste of their own medicine
quizExercises.explanation: 'Break the mould' means to be truly original and depart completely from convention.

quizExercises.sentence: I warned him that the sudden celebrity would ___, and sure enough, he became insufferable.
options: give him a taste of his own medicine
quizExercises.explanation: 'Go to your head' means to become arrogant or overly proud because of success or praise.

quizExercises.question: Which scenario describes someone 'sticking to their guns'?
options: A researcher changes their conclusions after pressure from funders
A researcher maintains their original findings despite pressure to alter them
A researcher abandons their hypothesis after initial tests fail
quizExercises.explanation: 'Stick to your guns' means to hold firm to your position or opinion despite opposition or pressure.

quizExercises.question: Which situation suggests someone is 'under another person's thumb'?
options: An employee who regularly challenges management decisions
An employee who never acts without their boss's approval and follows every instruction unquestioningly
An employee who frequently works independently on their own projects
quizExercises.explanation: 'Under sb's thumb' means to be controlled or dominated by someone, with no real independence.
